name: Rowan
description: The Rowan, or mountain ash, is a sacred tree in Celtic mythology, revered for its protective qualities and association with magic. Its bright red berries and delicate leaves made it a powerful symbol of life, regeneration, and warding off evil. Celts believed that Rowan possessed potent magical properties, offering protection against malevolent spirits, enchantments, and ill fortune. It was often planted near homes and sacred sites to provide a barrier against negative energies. Rowan wood was used to create protective amulets, staffs, and tools for divination. The berries, while slightly poisonous, were traditionally used in jams and jellies, believed to impart strength and vitality. Rowan's connection to the otherworld and its ability to bridge the gap between realms made it a vital component in Celtic ritual and folklore. It was seen as a symbol of courage, wisdom, and the power to overcome adversity. In some traditions, the Rowan is associated with female deities and the feminine aspect of nature. Its presence served as a reminder of the interconnectedness of all things and the enduring power of nature's magic. The tree's resilience in harsh environments further contributed to its symbolic strength and association with perseverance. The Rowan's place in Celtic lore underscores the deep respect and understanding the Celts had for the natural world and its inherent power.
subtitle: Protective tree of magic and life.

keyMyths.title: Rowan and the Goddess
keyMyths.description: A tale speaks of a goddess who transformed into a Rowan tree to escape a dark force, imbuing the tree with her protective powers. This myth highlights the Rowan's connection to divine protection.

sources.author: Miranda Green
sources.description: Discusses the role of sacred trees in Celtic religion.
sources.text: Discusses the role of sacred trees in Celtic religion.

sources.author: Peter Berresford Ellis
